channel.title: Tanut
channel.description: Tanut lives in Bangkok, Thailand. He is passionate about racing, programming, mechanical designs. This website is built to share his hobbies that might inspire someone

item.title: Box-Joint Generator for Laser Cutting
item.description:
Drawing a box joint outline on Illustrator one-by-one, size-by-size was a very boring task, so I created an automated script (written in Javascript/HTML) that generates box-joint in .svg format in a single click. This is how the program looks.

CLICK HERE

To go straight to the generator that runs on your browser.

  • You can try out different parameters like Thickness, Width, Height, and so on.
  • Click generate and the result object will be shown below.
  • Once you are satisfy with the result simple change ‘output’ to ‘svg’ and click generate again. This time .svg file will be downloaded.

item.title: Sim-Racing Pedal Attachment
item.description:
I had an issue with the height and angle of the accelerator/break pedals because they are too low and too deep in my seating setup, so I designed an attachment (the white plastic piece) which can be bolt on to Thrustmaster T3PA pedal to fix this issue. See youtube video below how I modified the pedals.

The attachment was 3d-printed using PLA plastic on Ender3 at the extrusion width of 0.3mm and 2 walls. Originally, the pedal is crew directly on to the printed plastic using M4 bolt but later I modified the design with M4 nut insert.

You can download the design this link https://a360.co/2ChwrGX (Autodesk Fusion 360)

item.title: Airfryer Potato
item.description:
I cooked potato fries with a new (cheap) airfryer. This is my first post using Jekyll + Barber + Github hosting.

Jekyll

Why did I choose Jekyll?

  • I didn’t much success writing on Wordpress. Imagine using MS Word, I often get overwhelmed by constantly changing in tab,font size,bullet point indentation, and etc. I would rather spending time setting it up then being annoyed by it.
  • Writing itself is already a challenging task for me who has a very difficult time in deducing my thoughts into comprehensible sentences. So, I was looking for something that takes plain text and styles it for me. Jekyll seems like a good solution that turns a plain text into an eye-pleasing page. An example of plain text of this post.
  • After hours of scrolling through many Jekyll themes, I found Barber Theme which looks so nice with a large collage on the homepage (at least something that I want for now).

Github

The next question that follows ‘how would I host my website?’ Hmmm… I looked around and found out that github can host a website. Imagine a cloud drive that not only keeps your code but also hosts your website for free. At first, it seems very simple to get index.html showing on the homepage but then pluging in Jekyll (officially supported) wasn’t so easy for me. It took me several hours.

My next challenge was to make it work with Barber Theme. This requires a work around because github doesn’t support auto-built on a custom themes. Here are steps I learnt from google search:

  1. With personal github account, Github.io always read index.html from ‘master’ branch, although in this tutorial it seems possible to choose other branches.
  2. Create branch ‘dev’ to store your Jekyll code.
  3. Code your website in branch ‘dev’. Once you are done, build the website using command ‘bundle exec jekyll serve’.
  4. Commit your new code and push to branch ‘dev’
  5. New website content will appear in folder ‘_site’. Copy them to somewhere outside i.e. ‘New folder’ on the desktop
  6. Change to branch ‘master’
  7. Delete everything, copy all content from ‘New folder’ and paste inside.
  8. Push changes to ‘master’

Wait for a couple of minutes and refresh your github.io website. You should be able to your new site! That’s a lot of manual steps. I wish it is easier but I’m pretty ok with it for now.
